sentences.text: Oh, yes, it changed a lot.
The war broke out, as everybody knows, the first of September.
And the Germans were very fast in coming into Poland.
So the third of September, three days later, we decided to run east, to run away towards the Russian border, towards Russia.
Because we know one thing, that young people-- we were young boys-- young people, is not good they should stay home.
They are-- nothing good was waiting for us.
Because number one, before 1939, he did-- with Polish Jews, he displaced them from Germany, to our town, to all the towns.
So we saw what Hitler means.
We knew what Hitler means.
Hitler means that the biggest enemy of the Jews.
And he has the power to do with them whatever he wants.
So my father said for the young people-- he gave me 100 zlotys, which was a lot, and I said goodbye.
And with other boy friends, a group of other boys, we start walking, just walking east.
Do you remember that goodbye?
Was that the last time you spoke with your father?
Well, I came back later.
I came back.
Yes, this was my mistake too.
When-- when I left and we start walking, we were walking for 18 days-- actually, for 15 days.
Because the 18th of September, we were on the Russian border.
We saw the Russian soldiers already.
No, they were in Russia.
We were on the border.
We looked through.
And we were ready any day, when the German army will cover Poland, that we will cross the border.
Because the international law is there is no borders.
During a war, there is no border.
They have to open the borders.
And they would open the borders.
But when we went to sleep the 18th of September, 1939, in the middle of the night, about 3, 4 o'clock in the morning, we heard tanks are coming.
Tanks are coming.
Instead, we go to Russia, the Russian army crossed the Polish border and came to Poland through their border.
We thought that they are going to help the Polish army to survive.
But there were a pact made between Hitler and Russia.
They take the western half of Poland and Russia take the eastern half of Poland.
And this was the story.
And suddenly, overnight, we are Russians.
We are no more Poles.
We are Russians.
Naturally, I-- we were on the border town, which is called Ostrog nad Horyniem.
This was the border.
We went back.
After being there a few days, what do we do in this small town?
We went to the big town, like, I would say, 20 or 25 miles west, Rovno.
This was already the big eastern Polish town.
And we started there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The money, the $100, wasn't going-- like, I lived for $1 a day.
They opened a kitchen.
We were called in Russian [RUSSIAN], displaced persons, which we didn't have homes.
We were without homes, [RUSSIAN].
But since the Jews in Rovno, very fine-- very fine Jews, with big hearts, everybody opened the door.
Everybody-- Because they were in the same bowl as we where.
Naturally, they saw in the meantime-- they were Russian, but they didn't know what was going on.
The Russians, in the beginning the army came in, the Russian army.
We had nothing to do with them.
But the first thing, I took-- I bought a Russian book, how to read  and how to write Russian.
Because we are going to be Russians.
And-- but right away, opened a [RUSSIAN], which is a market like.
Here they call it the-- these markets--
This-- no.
On the street, it's markets.
They're--
A flea market-- like a flea market.
But everybody had the [INAUDIBLE] on his hand.
And there are lines for the store.
I stand in the line.
What are they giving?
For 10 ruble, they gave you a piece of garbage fabric, cotton, which before the war I wouldn't want to look at it.
This was for a suit.
So I went out on this, right when this flea market.
And this was a nice story.
A soldier came to me, a Russian soldier-- what do you have for sale?
I say, I have material for a suit.
It was a flannel like.
How much is it?
So my-- my Russian-- well, I paid 10 ruble, so I figure I'll say 17 ruble.
So I say 17 in Russian. 70?
So 70?
He takes the piece of fabric from me, and he takes out a bunch of the 10-ruble pieces, and he gives me one, two, three, four, five, six, seven.
I have 70 already.
I look from heaven.
He thought the 70-- for them, the price meant nothing.
They had money, a lot of money, but they couldn't buy anything.
When they came to Poland, they had merchandise they bought.
They bought shoes.
In Russia, they couldn't buy anything.
Everything was on cards.
So it was a heaven for them.
They bought the watches, [RUSSIAN], one, two, three, four, five, six.
They could buy it for money.
You know, in Russia, they couldn't buy it for money.
In the times of Stalin, it was very bad, very tough in Russia.
Oh.

So it was-- it was a few month, till December, and they start to make-- at night they start the [NON-ENGLISH], or the [NON-ENGLISH], or whatever they were called then, picked up the [RUSSIAN], which we were-- the people without homes-- and took them to Russia to the coal mines.
So I--
Yeah, stories right away, in every second house.
So I was scared that some night they are coming to me, to mine-- to my house, and they will take me to the-- and to survive over there, I ran away from Hitler, and die in a coal mine in Russia.
This didn't like-- this is one.
And number two, boys were coming from my town to Rovno.
They were coming-- disappearing from there.
And finally, I found a boy, a neighbor of mine.
He said, oh, regards from your father.
I said, how is it at home?
He said, it's not so bad.
He goes Friday night to synagogue.
And the mother buys fish for Shabbos.
She makes challahs.
She-- it was a neighbor of ours.
And she lives-- naturally, he doesn't go out with his beard.
He shaved his beard, he said.
He-- because my father had a nice blond yellow beard.
And he shaved his beard.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And they live-- and now I said, what am I doing here?
I have a home.
I have a mother and a father.
Why do I sit here?
So I decided-- And that was the only information you had from what was going on back in--
Yes.
They-- naturally-- they shot one boy in our town.
At night he went out.
They shot him.
This was the whole story.
Otherwise, there was no ghetto.
There was no nothing.
Your mother goes to the stores with your sister.
Your father's afraid, because he has a beard.
This is the story.
I figured, they're almost a normal life.
And I'll go back home.
So I started to go back home.
So I'll tell you a little story on the way home.
We had to cross a river, Bug, the river Bug, to go back west to Poland, to my town, to Ostrowiec.
We crossed the river.
Naturally, there are people who you paid.
And they-- and they guide you how to cross and where to go.
You had to sneak across the border, correct?
You have to sneak, sure.
And I with another-- with another two boys.
Three boys we were.
And it was at night, like 10 o'clock at night, wintertime, December.
We went through the water.
We got wet through.
Then we saw a village.
We are in a village already, on the other side of the Bug, where the Germans are-- not the Russians, the German.
So we see a light.
We knocked at the door.
Oh, come in.
It was a Polish peasant.
I said, look, we just came back from the other side.
If we could sleep on the floor overnight, tomorrow we will leave.
If you could make us some hot tea and we can dry up our pants, we would appreciate it.
And we would pay you for this.
We have a few rubles to pay you, or Zlotys, rubles-- this was the same money.
So she said, oh yes, oh yes.
And-- and I'll put on tea for you, a kettle of tea.
And my husband-- he will go out for wood, to bring in more wood.
And he went out for wood.
It took 10, 15, 20 minutes.
He went out for wood.
And he came with the-- with a Wehrmacht soldier with a gun pointed to us.
Jews, you come.
And naturally, we were undressed already.
You put on the wet pants.
And for finding Jews, he got-- I understand they were told a kilo of sugar for that.
He A kilo of sugar for that.
So this was-- when we went to their headquarters, or the army headquarters, his leader, the officer, took out the gun.
And he said, look.
You came from there.
You go right back to this.
If you are not going back, you are going-- you are going to be dead.
And he shot in the air once.
And we were running down back to the Bug, to the river back-- naturally, 10, 11 o'clock at night, dark.
We were laying there for an hour.
And we went to another-- to another section, another village.
We saw there this section was called Polesie in Poland.
And very poor-- very poor people were there.
So we saw, again, a light in a window.
And we figure, it's quiet here.
It's far away already from-- and we knocked at the door.
This was a guy living by himself.
You wouldn't believe it.
The cow was standing in the corner.
He was sleeping here.
He had a table, a wooden table, with a kitchen.
All is the same story.
We ask him for some hot water.
He gave us some hot water.
He had milk.
So we paid him for the milk.
We slept over the night.
And the next day, we start our journey back home.
And my--
What?
No, that we-- just one night we were there.
And this was the night when we crossed the border.
When?
When are you talking about?
No, no.
I wanted to go home.
I didn't.
I wanted to go home.
I was ready to go home to my parents.
They said, we have the store.
We had everything.
It was normal life-- our apartment, you know?
So I figured, whoever thought about atrocities?
If you would behave yourself, you wouldn't do anything wrong, you would live.
When I got home, they were happy and unhappy, my parents.
Because there is no future here.
But they didn't tell me this.
Now, my family-- my father's brother, my two sisters-- came from Lodz.
They were displaced.
We had a-- suddenly, we had a big family in our town.
They were all displaced.
Naturally, I came too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My brother was displaced from Lodz and his wife and daughter.
My sister was with her husband and daughter and son displaced.
My other sister was displaced.
And my uncle-- my father's brother-- with his son was displaced.
They were-- because Lodz became Litzmannstadt, a part of the Reich, of the Third Reich.
And they displaced the Jews, leaving a small ghetto, about 1,500, which the famous Rumkowski was the leader of this ghetto.
They didn't want to stay in the ghetto.
And they ray away.
Because over there-- we-- they still live-- in our town, there was still freedom.
They still were walking and going out whenever they want.
German soldiers-- very few.
But what we saw is the Gestapo.
We had about five, six Gestapo headquarters.
And they were ruling the town.
They were the rulers of the town.
And naturally, it took some time.
We got in our store a [POLISH], which is a commissar.
She-- and my father didn't have the right mother to come into the store.
She hired me.
I should do with her the business.
I was the only one allowed to come into the store.
This was a good looking woman.
And she was a lover from the Gestapo.
So they gave her a gift.
They gave her three Jewish-- no, a Polish woman.
[POLISH] was her name, [POLISH], [POLISH].
And she was-- she had three stores, a few Jews-- as many as she wants, I think she could get.
But and so she picked our store.
Her headquarters was in our store.
And she took me.
And she gave me papers that I work for her.
And naturally, she was a bridge player too.
On the weekends, when we were closed-- Sunday we were closed, the stores, I spent the whole day in her house to play bridge.
And I had papers.
Sometimes, I played till 2, 12 o'clock midnight.
And a Jew couldn't walk more than, I think, 8:00 or 9:00-- whatever the curfew was.
And I came once back from her.
It was about 12 o'clock.
And who is going on the same side?
Mr.
[GERMAN], one of the Gestapo, with his dog, which was taller than I was.
And naturally, I stopped right away.
Show me your papers.
I showed him my papers.
He said, you can go.
If I wouldn't have the papers, either I got shot to death or-- he wouldn't bother to arrest me, at least not right away in the-- Were there other restrictions besides this curfew on Jews?
This came later, later after this.
As the time passed by, it started to be worse and worse for the Jews.
They-- people which they were living outside of the town had to come to the ghetto.
They picked up and they made a ghetto, let's say, about 12 street-- 10, 12 street in the poorest side of our town.
They had-- I have to take a drink of water.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Thank you.
[DRINKS] They started-- with the time, every month, every second month, came out newer laws, restrictions, restrictions.
There were times that we could go out from 11:00 to 12:00 in the morning an hour and 5:00 to 6:00 in the evening.
Just two hours a day we could be in the street.
Otherwise, we couldn't be in the street at all.
Oh, every Jew had to have a white band with a star, a white band with-- every Jew had to have it.
Their life changed, I think, for better.
Because-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like, my father had a store, right?
And we had a lot of merchandise.
What did we do with the merchandise, with the good stuff?
We gave to this Polack, to this Polack, to hide it.
Maybe, we will live through the war.
Maybe we will have, again, a store.
Hide it.
So they accepted the merchandise.
Why not?
Matter of fact, I-- it was my teacher, my teacher which she was our customer in the store too-- I myself brought her 50 men's shirts-- I myself.
After the war, I came to her.
I didn't have a shirt.
I asked her, can you give me a couple of shirts?
She says, no, nothing.
They're all-- the German took away everything.
You know what I mean?
So they-- a lot-- a lot of Polacks enriched themselves.
Another one told me like this-- after the war also, we gave a lot of merchandise.
I went to her.
Oh, your mother gave me all the [NON-ENGLISH] from the store.
But the gold, and the jewelry, the diamonds, she didn't give me.
Ah, you hear that?
This was the way.
She didn't give me.
She had such beautiful earrings and-- and her rings-- she didn't give me.
She gave me all the [NON-ENGLISH] from the store.
I say, give-- I would like to have a couple of shirts.
I'm sorry, I have nothing for you.
This was after the war.
So this was the point.
They didn't-- they didn't-- naturally, from time to times, if they didn't behave, they punished them too, but only if they did something wrong.
Oh, this was in '41.
And then, in '42, it starts.
In the middle of '42, I start to-- we start to hear that they are burning Jews, that there is a Treblinka and an Auschwitz, and that they are burning Jews.
A lot didn't believe.
A lot of Jews didn't believe.
And a lot did believe.
And then, they started the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] surrounding the ghettos and taking them at the railroads to the-- to the station, taking them with the railroad to Auschwitz.
So we start hearing this town was taken.
This town was taken.
Our town was not taken yet.
But I'm sure they will take from our town too.
Because from the small towns around Ostrowiec, all the Jews they pushed into Ostrowiec-- they should have them together.
Like, from 5,000, 6,000 Jews, suddenly, in '42, we had about 15,000 Jews in our town.
Sure, I believed.
And how I believed.
Number one, I was pessimistic.
I wasn't an optimist.
I didn't believe that God is going to save me if I'm not going to save myself.
So I was always pessimistic.
I saw what's coming.
I saw what's coming.
And I figured that, if I will be a salesman in the store, this is not going to help me.
I went to the steel mill.

And they hired-- they had about 1,500 Jews working there-- little, but they start with a few hundred, which the German-- the German government, the German from the town, told them they should take in Jews, number one.
The Jews didn't get no pay for their labor.
They just got a soup a day, which was slave labor.
Why not?
They took-- so they took the young people, the able people, to the steel mill.
I run by myself there.
I wanted to work there.
And since I had all the engineers and the meisters, which I knew-- we knew them-- there was no problem for me to get accepted.
And the commissar was mad at me.
He says, how come you're leaving such a good job?
I pay you good.
And you're leaving me alone with the stores.
And you're going-- I told her, this is not good enough for my life.
To save my life, I have to be in the mill.
A matter of fact, it was 1942.
September was the month, I guess, or the middle, or the end.
I don't remember which day.
It was in September '42.
Saturday, we saw a few hundred Ukrainian soldiers with Gestapo.
New faces came to town Saturday.
There I saw, this is it.
Sunday is going to be the ball.
So I didn't go to sleep at home.
And I said goodbye to my parents.
And I went to sleep in the steel mill, since over there I had 30 workers.
At night, I was sleeping in the office there by myself.
You weren't a worker?
You actually had people working for you?
Actually, I had 30 people working for me.
I gave them, in the morning-- the engineer told me what to do, the head engineer of the department.
We were in the-- called [POLISH].
We made-- we made these feathers to locomotives.
And it was-- we had the hot ovens.
It was a big, big-- quite a few hundred people were working there.
And I had the 30 Jews.
In every department, they had 30 or 50 Jews with the Polacks working.
I beg your pardon?
And how.
The same Sunday morning, early in the morning, at 7:00 in the morning, everybody had to leave the house.
And they took them to a marketplace, quite a few thousand Jews.
And in the afternoon, they start moving them to the railroad station.
And at this time, my father and mother and sister-- my father, mother, sister-- they were hiding in a attic.
And they missed them.
They missed-- this, they missed.
I was in the mill.
And they-- and they were hiding.
So after-- they were hiding a few days.
After a few days, they cleaned up the few thousand Jews.
It was quiet.
And they start to come out to the small ghetto.
And they cut away again.
They made a-- because they didn't have enough Jews already, that they made a small ghetto.
They cut away the streets.
And there were left about 1,500.
Naturally, my father was then-- and mother-- still alive.
And the few [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jews were hiding.
The rabbi, I think, was hiding there.
And they had-- the rabbi from the town.
We had a very famous rabbi-- and oh, his friends.
And they were sitting and studying the Talmud in the synagogue.
So I brought them some food, which I got the food from my coworker, the meister, the Polack, his wife.
He brought himself food.
He brought me also.
He brought me too.
I paid him for this.
So I brought it-- I brought it to my father and mother, to give them a little bit, that they should have what to eat.
They didn't-- they didn't have food.
Well, he didn't ask me if it's kosher or non-kosher.
And at this time, I said to my father that, you are sitting and studying Talmud.
Is this any help of you?
Does this give you any satisfaction?
He says, Moshe, who knows?
Maybe you are right.
I don't know any more.
Because he saw what was going on and what they were doing with the Jews and with the children and with the beautiful children, with the-- maybe, he says, you are right.
And it didn't take long.
